Jaguar  ·  E-Type — XJ-S — XK8/XKR — F-Type

NINE LIVES

One Jaguar bloodline, from the E-Type to the F-Type.And at its heart, the modern classic that refuses to fade — the XK8 & XKR.

March 11  ·  Opinions
The Proverb

When it comes to the term “Nine Lives,” no one is exactly sure where it comes from, but most agree that it derives from an old English proverb that talks about the three stages of existence using a cat for the analogy: “A cat has nine lives. For three he plays, for three he strays, and for three he stays.”

It is a beautiful piece of folk wisdom — deceptively simple on the surface, yet remarkably layered when you sit with it. The notion that any creature, or any great thing, moves through distinct chapters of its life, each with its own character and purpose, is something we recognize intuitively. Play, wander, then settle. Youth, restlessness, then belonging. It is the arc of nearly every great story ever told.

What is perhaps surprising is how perfectly that same arc maps onto a British sports car. But then again, the best automobiles have always been more than machines. They are characters. They have temperaments, histories, second acts. And few have lived as many lives — or lived them as gracefully — as the Jaguar XK8 and XKR.

But no life is the first, and no cat is born in a vacuum. To understand why the XK8 mattered — why it carried the weight it did — you have to go back, past the difficult middle child that raised it, all the way to the one that started everything. The most beautiful car ever made.

1961 · The Firstborn

The Firstborn Added

When Sir William Lyons pulled the covers off the E-Type at Geneva in March of 1961, he did not just launch a car — he set a standard that every Jaguar since has been quietly, desperately trying to live up to. This is the one. The firstborn. The impossibly beautiful older brother who was good at everything, whom everyone loved, and whose shadow the rest of the family has spent sixty years trying to step out of.

The legend is almost too good to be true, except that it is. Jaguar had exactly one demonstrator at the show and demand for rides was so ferocious that test driver Norman Dewis was told to get the second car, “77 RW,” to Geneva — now. He drove it through the night, flat out across Europe, and arrived at dawn. That is the kind of story that gets invented after the fact to sell a legend. In this case it actually happened.

And then there is the quote. Enzo Ferrari — the man who built the most desirable cars on earth, who had no reason on God’s green earth to hand a compliment to a rival — reportedly took one look and called it the most beautiful car ever made. Whether he said it word for word or not almost does not matter. The point is that it was believable coming from him. That is how good it was.

But here is what the postcard-pretty version leaves out, and here is where the E-Type actually changed the industry: it was not styled. Not really. It was calculated. Malcolm Sayer, the man who drew it, was not a stylist chasing a pretty shape — he was an aerodynamicist who came out of the aircraft world, and he shaped that body with mathematics and airflow, not with a designer’s ego. The most sensual, most openly erotic form ever put on four wheels was, underneath, an engineering solution. That was the lesson Coventry taught the world in 1961: beauty and function were never enemies. Do the engineering properly and the beauty falls out of it.

Then there was the money. A hundred and fifty miles per hour — genuinely, or near enough that the magazines printed it — for a fraction of what Ferrari or Aston Martin were asking. The E-Type took the exotic, the toy of princes and playboys, and put it within reach of a successful doctor or an ambitious young man who wanted to announce he had arrived. It democratized desire. Every attainable performance car that followed, right down to the ones we drive today, owes something to that idea.

“Not a car in a museum. A sculpture that happened to do 150.”

Monocoque construction borrowed from the D-Type racer. Disc brakes on all four corners when most cars still had drums, because Jaguar had already proven them at Le Mans. Independent rear suspension so sophisticated it went straight into the saloons. This was not style over substance — it was both, at once, and that combination is exactly why the Museum of Modern Art in New York eventually put one behind glass as a permanent exhibit. Not a car in a museum. A sculpture that happened to do 150.

That is the brother the others grew up under. That is the act nobody could follow. And when Jaguar finally had to try — when the E-Type’s time was up and something had to take its place — they did not even attempt to out-pretty it. They went the other way entirely. They built the middle child.

1975 · The Middle Child

The Middle Child

The XJ-S is not a sporty car, was never supposed to be, it was always more of a GT car that never really captured the essence, passion, or pure magic of the E-Type it was meant to replace. It is still captivating, it just does not have that allure of the XKE or its replacement the XK8/R. It was much harder angled, much more menacing than the art form of its two siblings, it was definitely the middle child. Different, had to stand out on its own, did not want to follow or conform to history or society. It has now grown into an elegant beauty of its own right perfectly positioned between the sculpted timeless beauty of its older brother and youthful, energetic, pretty boy of its younger brother. It’s almost like the siblings skip a generation as the F-Type is more of a direct descendant of the XJ-S even though that was more of a sports car rather than a GT.

“Much harder angled, much more menacing than the art form of its two siblings.”

1996 · The Return of Beauty

The Pretty Boy

If the E-Type was the firstborn and the XJ-S the difficult middle child, the XK8 was the one that finally inherited the family’s looks — and, unlike its brooding older brother, it knew exactly what it was for.

Born into Controversy

The XK8 and XKR, now 25 years of age, perfectly describe the three stages of having nine lives. Born from the controversial XJS — which was itself marketed as a poor replacement for the brilliant and exquisite XKE — the XK had to pounce right out of the gate in order not to be considered the runt of the litter. The shadow it was stepping out of was immense. The XKE, or E-Type as it is known in Britain, is widely considered one of the most beautiful automobiles ever designed. Enzo Ferrari himself called it “the most beautiful car ever made.” Following that act was never going to be easy.

The XJS, which bridged the gap between the E-Type era and the XK generation, was always a polarizing machine. Launched in 1975, it arrived into a world gripped by an oil crisis, and its grand touring proportions and thirsty V12 felt tone-deaf to some. Critics were harsh. Enthusiasts were divided. Yet it endured for 21 years — a tenure that speaks more to its quiet appeal than any review ever captured. Still, by the time Jaguar was ready to replace it in 1996, the pressure was immense: produce something that genuinely deserves to wear the XK badge, or risk becoming irrelevant.

Around that same time, Jaguar faced its own evolution as a company. They went from independently owned, to incorporated under the British Leyland Group, back to independently owned, to staring down the barrel of a General Motors acquisition, to finally being saved by Ford. It was a corporate saga as dramatic as anything on the road — a company lurching between identities, between owners, between near-collapses and unlikely reprieves. What is remarkable is that through all of it, the soul of Jaguar — that instinct for beauty, for pace, for a certain kind of effortless cool — was never fully extinguished.

It was during all this upheaval that Jaguar produced the brilliant first-generation XK8 and XKR. Putting aside the XJR-15 and XJ220, masterpieces that only a handful of people could ever own, the XK8 and XKR were the only Jaguars worthy of the mantle once carried by the sexy and seductive XKE. When the XK8 debuted at the Geneva Motor Show in March 1996, it stopped the room. Here, at last, was a Jaguar that looked the way a Jaguar was supposed to look.

Form as Philosophy

Even at a standstill, the XK radiates speed. Its sleek, athletic contours and powerful rear haunches create a striking visual energy that most modern cars, with their software-generated creases and committee-approved proportions, simply cannot replicate. Speed has never been this captivating.

The design was the work of Geoff Lawson, Jaguar’s Director of Styling, whose brief was essentially to do justice to the bloodline. Lawson succeeded by looking both backward and forward simultaneously — drawing on the long hood, flowing roofline, and sensual curves that defined the E-Type, while giving the car a contemporary muscularity and presence. Every surface flows into the next with an almost liquid confidence. There is no aggression for aggression’s sake, no unnecessary hard angles or theatrical vents. It is a car that communicates through restraint.

The alluring dynamic visual arises from an aluminum skin paired with a lightweight yet robust body structure. The design prioritizes aerodynamic efficiency, ensuring the promise of exhilarating performance and driving thrill. It is the strong, lightweight aluminum framework that is so essential for its agility and dynamic handling. This was progressive engineering for its era — Jaguar was one of the first mainstream manufacturers to commit to aluminum construction at this scale, reducing weight and improving rigidity in ways that directly served the driving experience.

The interior matched the exterior’s ambition. Step inside and you are greeted by a cabin that feels handcrafted rather than assembled. The classic wooden steering wheel sits at the center of a dashboard layered in genuine burr walnut veneer, the kind of material that takes craftsmen hours to select and fit. Leather wraps everything with purpose and precision, finely stitched and color-matched with an attention to detail that speaks of an older industrial tradition — one in which the people making the car were also proud of it.

The Art of Controlled Power

With an all-new aluminum V-8 engine designed solely by Jaguar — not Ford, which owned the company at the time — the XK8 produced a very potent 290 horsepower and 290 lb-ft of torque. For the supercharged XKR, those figures climbed to a formidable 370 horsepower and 387 lb-ft. The engine, internally designated the AJ-V8, was a genuine point of pride for Coventry’s engineers. Jaguar had long relied on XK-derived straight-six engines, and the move to a new, purpose-built V-8 was a statement of intent: this was not a company in retreat, but one pressing forward.

The XK8 was capable of getting you and your co-pilot to 60 mph in a heart-pumping 6.2 seconds. The XKR did it in 4.9 seconds. At the time, this was rapid — and the AJ-V8 was considered one of the finest engines being produced anywhere in the world. Powerful, smooth, and utterly refined at any speed, it delivered its performance without drama or coarseness. Even when tip-toeing the pedal down, the XK pounces into action as if it were ambushing its prey.

The sensation of the roar and effortlessness of the engine travels through the elongated hood, past the elegant steering wheel, and into the leather-wrapped seats, comforting and securing you in total bliss. It is a full sensory experience — the low, authoritative note of the exhaust under load, the barely perceptible vibration through the steering wheel, the surge of forward momentum that feels both immediate and entirely composed. This is not a car that shouts at you. It whispers, and somehow the whisper is more compelling.

The XK excels at being both agile and composed. Like most cats, you shouldn’t let its size and weight fool you. The XK is well-planted and ready to embrace the corners on mountainous or canyon roads. The handling is well-balanced and predictable, and it rides unbelievably well thanks to its suspension — firm enough to give the driver the feedback we all yearn for, that tangibility, that visceral feel you want, yet supple enough for soaking up any potholes you might discover along the way. Much of this is owed to the highly sophisticated CATS (Computer Active Technology Suspension) system, which continuously adjusts damper rates to suit conditions, threading the needle between sport and comfort with a deftness that still impresses today.

What It Actually Feels Like

But let’s be honest. Most people are not purchasing an XK specifically, or even a Jaguar generally, for the superior handling dynamics. Most people purchase them because it makes them feel elegant. The thrill you get when you are driving down a city street and catch your reflection in a glass building. Or when you are top-down on the freeway and catch other drivers craning their necks to see who’s in the driver’s seat. Their design, their allure, and how they make you feel.

Your body produces a surge of endorphins. Your confidence rises. You become enchanted. You feel like you have life figured out. You see the person you have always fantasized about becoming, and you automatically become that person. It sounds hyperbolic until you’ve experienced it. There is something about a beautiful car that flatters its driver in a way that no other luxury object quite does — not a watch, not a suit, not a fine piece of furniture. A car moves through the world with you. It is the only luxury that participates.

Jaguars have a very unique ability to sweep you away and create raw emotion that few vehicles can. And the XK does this in strides. Teeming with luxury literally fit for royalty — from the classic wooden steering wheel to the finely stitched leather encompassing the car like a glove — the XK has grown into a modern classic. With its absence of GPS, Bluetooth, and bright digital screens, it becomes like a time machine taking you back to a time when everything wasn’t so connected. When you had to know where you were going, or at least enjoy getting lost along the way. When driving meant engaging with your senses and not the algorithm.

It has everything you need and nothing you don’t. You’re not going to win any races or out-corner any modern SUV on the market. But that is not what owning an XK is about. It is for cruising down a charming backroad in New England during the fall with the wind in your hair, or chasing that sunset on the ocean cliffs. It’s for watching the autumn leaves in your rearview mirror as you see them dance in your wake on a mountain road. Owning the XK is about rolling up to the valet on a Friday evening and knowing they are going to park it out front. It’s hearing the history of the Le Mans wins every time you turn that key.

Three He Plays, Three He Strays, Three He Stays

The XK, back in the late nineties and early two-thousands, was one of the most desired vehicles one could obtain. It occupied a rarefied space in the market — grand enough to feel truly luxurious, athletic enough to feel genuinely sporting, and beautiful enough to attract attention from people who could not care less about horsepower figures. It sold well. It won awards. It appeared in films and on magazine covers. For a moment, Jaguar was back.

Naturally, as time progressed, the market went in a different direction. A second generation was offered in 2006, redesigned under Ford’s direction to be more modern, more powerful, and more technologically advanced. It gained in capability what it sacrificed in charm. The new car was wider, lower, and sharper-edged — undeniably competent, but somehow less poetic. It just didn’t have the same grace or caché as the first generation. The magic that Geoff Lawson had conjured was not something that could simply be refreshed. It was of its moment.

Eventually, the XK moved aside and made room for a new generation of Jaguars — the F-Pace SUV and F-Type — designed to keep pace with the growing number of luxury sports cars and crossovers becoming available in the market. Jaguar needed volume. It needed relevance in a world pivoting toward utility vehicles and electrification. The XK was a product of a different era’s ambitions.

But the first-generation XK8 and XKR have found their place on the Jaguar pedestal alongside the greats: the D-Type, the XKSS, the XK120, the XK140, the XK150, the E-Type, the XJR-15, and the XJ220. Sure, it found its way by going a different route — one not fortified in racing, but in design, style, and desire. Yet always in keeping with Jaguar elegance. Always, unmistakably, a Jaguar.

Today, a well-kept first-generation XK8 or XKR can be found for a fraction of what it cost new — sometimes as little as $10,000 to $20,000 for a solid example. That pricing is, frankly, extraordinary for what you are getting. It will not stay that way forever. The collector market has already begun to take notice. As the generation that grew up coveting these cars reaches the age and income to act on that desire, values will move. The window to acquire a piece of genuine automotive history at an accessible price is open — but not indefinitely.

The Cat That Stays

The nine lives proverb ends not with play or wandering, but with staying. That is where the XK8 and XKR are now. They have found their people — enthusiasts who understand what they are, who are not chasing spec sheets or lap times, but something less quantifiable and far more lasting. These are cars owned by people who drive them to farmer’s markets on Sunday mornings, who take them on road trips without itineraries, who wash them by hand because they have earned that ritual.

There is a certain kind of automotive appreciation that requires patience. Not every great car announces itself immediately. Some need time to separate from the noise of their era, to shed the baggage of their period reviews and market pressures, to be seen clearly. The XK8 and XKR are there now. They are seen.

“They played. They strayed. And now, undeniably, they stay.”

Jaguar XK8 / XKR
Technical Specifications
Engine & Drivetrain
Engine TypeV8, Aluminum, DOHC
Displacement256.1 cu-in / 4,196 cc
Bore x Stroke3.39 x 3.56 in / 86.0 x 90.3 mm
Valves32 Valves (4 per cylinder)
AspirationNaturally Aspirated (XKR: Eaton M112 Supercharger)
Compression Ratio11.0:1 (XKR: 9.1:1)
Horsepower290 HP / 221 kW @ 6,000 rpm  │  XKR: 370 HP / 294 kW @ 6,100 rpm
Torque290 lb-ft / 420 Nm @ 4,100 rpm  │  XKR: 387 lb-ft / 550 Nm @ 3,500 rpm
Transmission6-Speed Automatic (ZF)
DriveRear-Wheel Drive
Fuel SystemMulti-Point Injection (MPI)
Performance
0–60 mph6.2 seconds  │  XKR: 4.9 seconds
0–100 km/h6.5 seconds  │  XKR: 5.4 seconds
Top Speed155 mph / 250 km/h (electronically limited)
Power-to-Weight5.6 kg/hp (XK8)
Dimensions & Weight
Wheelbase101.97 in / 259.0 cm
Length188.19 in / 478.0 cm
Width72.05 in / 183.0 cm
Height51.18 in / 130.0 cm
Curb Weight3,715 lbs / 1,685 kg
Trunk Capacity11.5 cu-ft / 327 L
Suspension, Brakes & Tires
Front SuspensionIndependent Double Wishbones, Coil Springs, Anti-Roll Bar
Rear SuspensionMulti-Link, Coil Springs
Active SuspensionCATS (Computer Active Technology Suspension)
Front BrakesVented Discs — 12.8 in / 325 mm
Rear BrakesVented Discs — 12.01 in / 305 mm
Tire Size (F/R)245/50 R17
Fuel Economy & Range
City (US)14 MPG  │  XKR: 13 MPG
Highway (US)28 MPG  │  XKR: 26 MPG
Combined (US)21 MPG  │  XKR: 19 MPG
Fuel Tank19.8 gallons / 75 L
Range412 miles / 663 km  │  XKR: 375 miles
CO₂ Emissions284 g/km
1992 · The Outlier

The Ninth Life Added

Every cat gets nine lives, and Jaguar spent one of its strangest and best on this. Between the middle child and the pretty boy, almost as a dare, the company built the XJ220 — for a brief, glorious window the fastest production car in the world. It was promised as a V12 with four-wheel drive and delivered as a twin-turbo V6 with the rear wheels doing all the work, which started lawsuits and broke a few hearts, but none of that matters now. What matters is 217 miles per hour, a shape drawn by the wind, and the sound of a company that had spent decades building beautiful GTs suddenly reminding everyone it still knew how to build a weapon. It did not fit the family. That was the whole point. The odd one out. The proof that the claws were always still there.

2013 · The One Who Came Back

The One Who Came Back Added

And that last thought deserves to be chased down, because it is the most interesting part of the whole family tree. On paper, the XK8 and its supercharged R sibling are the E-Type’s heirs — round, sensual, curved, openly pretty. Geoff Lawson drew the XK8 as a love letter to the E-Type, and you can see it in every soft radius of the thing. But that is precisely the point. The XK8 inherited the E-Type’s beauty. It did not inherit the E-Type’s nerve.

The F-Type, coupe and convertible. Even standing still it looks mid-pounce — the muscular haunches and hooded lights are pure XJ-S menace, finally let off the leash and given a supercharged V8 to make it audible.

The XJ-S did something braver. It refused to trade on the past. It stood in the shadow of the most beautiful car ever made and decided, deliberately, not to imitate it — to be harder, stranger, more menacing, to make people argue about it rather than fall instantly in love. And for a while that looked like a mistake. For years the XJ-S was the awkward one, the difficult one, the sibling nobody quite knew what to do with.

Then came the F-Type, and suddenly the resemblance was obvious. Not to the pretty XK8, its immediate predecessor — but to the XJ-S, a generation further back. Ian Callum, the man who shaped it, was clear from the start that the F-Type would not be a retro tribute, would not cosplay as an E-Type, would not survive on nostalgia. It had to earn its place on its own terms — the exact same instinct that drove the XJ-S thirty-odd years earlier. Look at the F-Type and you see it: the muscular haunches over the rear wheels, the aggressive angles, the sense that the thing is spoiling for a fight rather than posing for a postcard. That is not the E-Type’s sensuality. That is the XJ-S’s menace, dormant for a generation, come roaring back with a supercharged V8 snarl to make it audible.

So yes — the siblings skipped a generation. The E-Type’s looks went to the XK8. But the E-Type’s courage, its refusal to conform, its insistence on being its own thing, skipped right past the pretty boy and landed in the middle child, and waited, and then reappeared in the youngest and angriest of them all. The F-Type is not the XK8’s little brother. It is the XJ-S grown up, finally understood, and finally, gloriously, unafraid.

“The XJ-S grown up, finally understood, and finally, gloriously, unafraid.”
